title: The calculator and the teacher
summary: A Manchester maths teacher decides which classes use calculators and which do not — and a colleague pushes back.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Helen, you have been doing this nineteen years. I am asking honestly — why do you ban calculators in Year Eight?

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: I do not ban them. I delay them. They come back in Year Nine. The two terms in between are when I want students to feel arithmetic in their hands.

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: If you have multiplied 27 by 14 by hand, you know — with your body, not with your head — roughly what that answer should look like. About 380. When a student later types 27 times 14 into a calculator and reads 3,780, they should feel uncomfortable. Most of mine do.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: So you are training a kind of alarm.

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: Yes. The calculator is a fine tool. It is a terrible first language. If you learn arithmetic through the calculator, you trust whatever it shows. You will not catch your own typos.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: But the new curriculum allows calculators from Year Seven.

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: I know. I have read it. The curriculum allows. It does not require. I have asked the department to keep my two-term delay as a local exception. So far they have.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Some parents complain that their children fall behind in tests with arithmetic-by-hand.

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: A few do. I show them the Year Ten data. My students, after the calculator returns, perform slightly better, not worse, on the timed tests. The gap is small but consistent. I have seven years of it.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Would you make the same case for spelling and dictionaries?

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: Differently. Spelling is more arbitrary than arithmetic. I think you can learn to write well with the dictionary always open. I do not think you can learn to estimate well with the calculator always open. The two skills are not symmetrical.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: What about the calculator on a phone? It is in every pocket already.

body.speaker: Helen
body.text: It is. That is exactly why the two terms matter. Once it is in your pocket forever, you need to have had two terms when it was not.

annotations.meaningVi: số học (tính cộng trừ nhân chia)
chuông báo / tín hiệu cảnh báo
ngôn ngữ đầu tiên tồi tệ (ẩn dụ)
tuỳ tiện / võ đoán (không theo quy luật rõ)
đối xứng (ở đây nghĩa: tương tự)

grammarNotes.title: First conditional vs. second conditional in argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tranh luận, ta thường chuyển giữa first conditional (có thể xảy ra) và second conditional (giả định, ít thực hơn). First conditional: 'If you learn arithmetic through the calculator, you trust whatever it shows. You will not catch your own typos.' — Helen mô tả một quy luật có thể xảy ra. Second conditional thường dùng 'If + past simple, would + V': 'I think you could learn to write well with the dictionary always open' (ẩn 'if you tried'). Ở B1, hãy chú ý: first conditional dùng để dự đoán; second conditional dùng cho tình huống giả định hoặc không thực hiện được hoàn toàn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Argument moves between first conditional (likely future) and second conditional (hypothetical). First conditional: 'If you learn arithmetic through the calculator, you trust whatever it shows. You will not catch your own typos.' — Helen describes a likely outcome. Second conditional uses 'If + past simple, would + V': 'I think you could learn to write well with the dictionary always open' (implicit 'if you tried'). At B1, first conditional = likely; second conditional = hypothetical or contrary to fact.

translationVi:
Ravi: Helen, chị đã làm việc này mười chín năm. Tôi hỏi thật — sao chị cấm máy tính ở Lớp 8?
Helen: Tôi không cấm. Tôi trì hoãn. Chúng quay lại Lớp 9. Hai học kỳ ở giữa là khi tôi muốn học sinh cảm nhận số học bằng tay mình.
Ravi: Cảm nhận thế nào?
Helen: Nếu đã nhân 27 với 14 bằng tay, em biết — bằng thân thể, không phải bằng đầu — đáp án nên trông cỡ nào. Khoảng 380. Khi một học sinh sau này gõ 27 nhân 14 vào máy tính và đọc 3.780, em ấy phải thấy khó chịu. Đa số học sinh của tôi cảm thấy thế.
Ravi: Vậy chị đang huấn luyện một loại chuông báo.
Helen: Đúng. Máy tính là công cụ tốt. Nó là ngôn ngữ đầu tiên tồi tệ. Nếu học số học qua máy tính, em tin bất cứ gì nó hiện. Em sẽ không bắt được lỗi gõ của chính mình.
Ravi: Nhưng chương trình mới cho phép máy tính từ Lớp 7.
Helen: Tôi biết. Tôi đọc rồi. Chương trình cho phép. Không bắt buộc. Tôi xin khoa giữ trì hoãn hai học kỳ của tôi như ngoại lệ địa phương. Đến giờ họ giữ.
Ravi: Một số phụ huynh phàn nàn rằng con tụt lại trong các bài kiểm tra số học bằng tay.
Helen: Một số ít. Tôi đưa họ xem dữ liệu Lớp 10. Học sinh tôi, sau khi máy tính quay lại, làm tốt hơn một chút, không tệ hơn, trong bài kiểm tra có thời gian. Khoảng cách nhỏ nhưng nhất quán. Tôi có bảy năm dữ liệu.
Ravi: Chị có lập luận tương tự cho chính tả và từ điển không?
Helen: Khác đi. Chính tả võ đoán hơn số học. Tôi nghĩ ta có thể học viết tốt với từ điển luôn mở. Tôi không nghĩ ta học ước lượng tốt với máy tính luôn mở. Hai kỹ năng không đối xứng.
Ravi: Còn máy tính trên điện thoại? Nó đã trong mọi túi rồi.
Helen: Đúng. Chính vì thế hai học kỳ đó quan trọng. Một khi nó trong túi mãi mãi, em cần có hai học kỳ khi nó không có ở đó.

questions.prompt: How long has Helen been teaching?

options: She delays them — they return in Year Nine after two terms
She allows them from Year Seven

questions.prompt: Why should multiplying by hand matter, in her view?
options: So students later feel uncomfortable when a calculator gives a wrong-looking answer (e.g. 3,780 instead of ~380)

questions.prompt: What is Helen training, in Ravi's words?
What does she say about the curriculum?
options: It allows calculators from Year Seven but does not require them; she has a local exception
It requires them in all years

questions.prompt: How does Helen handle parental complaints?
options: Shows them Year Ten data — her students perform slightly better, gap small but consistent, seven years

questions.prompt: How does Helen compare arithmetic and spelling?
options: Not symmetrical — you can learn to write well with dictionary open, but not to estimate well with calculator open

questions.prompt: What does Helen say about phones in pockets?
options: Because the calculator is in every pocket forever, the two terms without it matter more, not less

questions.prompt: What metaphor does Helen use for calculators?
options: A fine tool but a terrible first language

questions.prompt: What does she think a calculator-trained student cannot do?

options: She has seven years of data
A teacher delays a powerful tool not to reject it, but to build a bodily sense of arithmetic that lets students later question the tool's answers
Spelling is more important than maths

cloze.template:
Ravi: Helen, you have been doing this nineteen years. I am asking honestly — why do you ban calculators in Year Eight?
Helen: I do not ban them. I {{b1}} them. They come back in Year Nine. The two terms in between are when I want students to feel arithmetic in their hands.
Ravi: Feel it how?
Helen: If you have multiplied 27 by 14 by hand, you know — with your body, not with your head — roughly what that answer should look like. About 380. When a student later types 27 times 14 into a calculator and reads 3,780, they should feel {{b2}}. Most of mine do.
Ravi: So you are training a kind of alarm.
Helen: Yes. The calculator is a fine tool. It is a terrible first {{b3}}. If you learn arithmetic through the calculator, you trust whatever it shows. You will not catch your own {{b4}}.
Ravi: But the new curriculum allows calculators from Year Seven.
Helen: I know. I have read it. The curriculum allows. It does not require. I have asked the department to keep my two-term delay as a local {{b5}}. So far they have.
Ravi: Some parents complain that their children fall behind in tests with arithmetic-by-hand.
Helen: A few do. I show them the Year Ten data. My students, after the calculator returns, perform slightly better, not worse, on the timed tests. The gap is small but {{b6}}. I have {{b7}} years of it.
Ravi: Would you make the same case for spelling and dictionaries?
Helen: Differently. Spelling is more arbitrary than arithmetic. I think you can learn to write well with the dictionary always open. I do not think you can learn to {{b8}} well with the calculator always open. The two skills are not symmetrical.
Ravi: What about the calculator on a phone? It is in every pocket already.
Helen: It is. That is exactly why the two {{b9}} matter. Once it is in your pocket forever, you need to have had two terms when it was {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: When it was not (in your pocket).

criticalThinkingQuestion: Helen argues a tool used too early can stop you from sensing wrong answers later. Pick a tool you depend on (a translator app, a maps app, a spell-checker). What would two months without it teach you, and what would two years without it cost you?
